summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the Windows clipboard's single-item limitation is an outdated design that fails to match modern computing capabilities. Despite having powerful hardware in 2005, users still lose clipboard contents when copying new items. He advocates for a simple FIFO queue system that would preserve the clipboard's ease of use while adding power-user functionality. He recommends the lightweight utility clcl as a workaround and expresses disappointment that Vista apparently won't address this fundamental limitation.
key_points: The single-item clipboard is an archaic limitation that doesn't match modern hardware capabilities
A FIFO queue of clipboard items would add power without complicating basic usage
The proliferation of third-party clipboard utilities proves this functionality should be built into the OS
Simple improvements like clipboard history persistence across reboots dramatically improve workflow
The clipboard's simplicity is admirable but has crossed the line into being too simple
money_quotes: In this era of 3ghz processors, 1gb memory, and 500gb hard drives, why is the Windows clipboard only capable of holding a single item?
Adding a basic FIFO queue of clipboard items wouldn't affect typical usage – but it would provide much richer functionality for intermediate and advanced users.
The clipboard is a heck of a lot more useful to me when I don't have to constantly worry about losing the one item on it.
The clipboard is a model of simplicity. And that's admirable. But I think it's too simple.
To me, that's is a sign that better clipboard functionality really should be built into the operating system.
It'd be a darn shame if we're stuck with the archaic single item clipboard for another five years.
key_insight: When dozens of third-party utilities exist to solve the same basic problem, that's a clear signal the functionality should be built into the operating system itself.
